impacts.caseName: Teva Pharmaceuticals International GmbH v. Eli Lilly and Company
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court erred in granting judgment as a matter of law that Teva's headache patents claiming methods of using humanized anti-CGRP antagonist antibodies to treat headache were invalid for failure to satisfy the written-description and enablement requirements of 35 U.S.C. § 112.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's JMOL of invalidity of Teva's headache patents, which cover methods of treating headache using humanized anti-CGRP antagonist antibodies (the technology behind Teva's Ajovy product). A jury had found willful infringement by Lilly and no invalidity, but the district court overturned the verdict. This decision restores Teva's patent protection in the lucrative migraine treatment market.
impacts.positiveImplications: Strengthens patent holders' ability to obtain and enforce claims to methods of using known classes of biologics for therapeutic purposes, particularly where the specification discloses one embodiment and references known methods for producing others.
impacts.negativeImplications: Competitors developing anti-CGRP antibody therapies face renewed infringement risk; the decision may embolden broader genus claims in the biopharmaceutical space, potentially raising barriers to entry.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder that conditions patent licenses on exclusive purchase of unpatented products engages in unlawful tying under the Sherman Act when those unpatented products have substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act. Ingevity conditioned licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' exclusive purchase of Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the jury's finding that the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses (e.g., air-intake systems outside the patent's scope), precluding Ingevity from using patent law as a shield against antitrust liability.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors benefit from the holding that patent holders cannot leverage patent licenses to compel exclusive purchases of unpatented goods with substantial non-infringing uses, preserving competitive markets for downstream products.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face significant antitrust exposure when structuring license agreements that tie unpatented product purchases to patent rights; conditioning licenses on exclusive dealing may constitute per se or rule-of-reason tying violations.

impacts.caseName: Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming methods for detecting anomalous program executions by comparing function calls in an emulator to a model of expected function calls are directed to patent-ineligible abstract ideas under 35 U.S.C. § 101, and whether damages may be awarded based on foreign sales.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million jury verdict for Columbia, holding that the asserted claims of the '322 and '115 patents are abstract at Alice step one and remanding for step two analysis. The court also held that the district court erred in allowing damages based on Norton's foreign sales. This decision significantly narrows the enforceability of cybersecurity method patents and limits extraterritorial patent damages.
impacts.positiveImplications: Defendants in patent suits involving software/cybersecurity methods gain a stronger basis for § 101 challenges; the foreign sales ruling limits patentees' ability to collect damages on overseas revenue.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in cybersecurity and software fields face heightened § 101 invalidity risk; university and research institution patent portfolios directed to detection/comparison methods may be devalued.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming server-side methods for tailoring content delivery specifications to a wireless device's screen size and capabilities are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's patents claiming methods of tailoring content delivery to device capabilities are ineligible under § 101, reversing a $2.5 million jury verdict in GoTV's favor. The court found the claims directed to the abstract idea of adapting content presentation to device characteristics. This eliminates GoTV's infringement recovery against Netflix and reinforces § 101 as a potent defense in streaming technology patent disputes.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming and content delivery companies benefit from a strong precedent shielding adaptive bitrate and device-tailored content delivery systems from patent infringement claims.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with portfolios directed to content adaptation and streaming optimization face increased § 101 vulnerability, potentially devaluing significant patent assets.

impacts.caseName: VLSI Technology LLC v. Intel Corporation
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder can establish infringement under the doctrine of equivalents for multicore processor selection methods when the accused processes occur predominantly outside the United States, and whether the district court erred in striking the patentee's damages expert theories.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed in part the district court's grant of summary judgment of noninfringement of the '836 patent on extraterritoriality grounds, holding that genuine issues of material fact existed as to whether sufficient infringing conduct occurred domestically. The court affirmed summary judgment on doctrine of equivalents grounds. The decision clarifies the territorial reach of U.S. method patents in the semiconductor industry.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders asserting method claims against global semiconductor manufacturers gain clarity that domestic acts of infringement can support liability even when parts of the process occur abroad.
impacts.negativeImplications: Semiconductor and technology companies with globally distributed manufacturing and design processes face continued uncertainty about the territorial scope of U.S. method patent liability.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether summary judgment was proper on inequitable conduct and Walker Process antitrust fraud claims relating to patents on quenched-and-tempered coiled tubing where the applicant acquired prior art documents describing a predecessor product.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgments on both inequitable conduct (granted for Global Tubing) and Walker Process fraud (granted for Tenaris), finding genuine disputes of material fact on both issues. The court held that questions remained about whether Tenaris's failure to disclose predecessor CYMAX documents to the PTO was intentionally deceptive and whether the fraud elements for antitrust liability were met.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies accused of inequitable conduct benefit from the court's insistence on genuine factual inquiry into intent to deceive, preventing automatic unenforceability findings based on acquisition of prior art documents.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that acquire assets including prior art documents face litigation risk if they fail to disclose those documents during patent prosecution; the revival of Walker Process claims creates antitrust exposure tied to patent procurement fraud.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the International Trade Commission correctly found that Apple's importation and sale of Apple Watch models with blood oxygen measurement functionality violated Section 337 of the Tariff Act by infringing Masimo's patents on wearable pulse oximetry devices.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's limited exclusion order barring importation of Apple Watch models incorporating blood oxygen sensing features that infringe Masimo's patents. The court upheld the Commission's domestic industry determination, validity rulings, and infringement findings. This decision sustains a significant import ban on one of Apple's flagship product lines.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in the medical device and wearable technology space are reinforced in their ability to use ITC proceedings to obtain exclusion orders against major technology companies importing infringing products.
impacts.negativeImplications: Major technology companies integrating health-monitoring features into consumer electronics face significant import ban risk from ITC proceedings brought by specialized medical device patent holders.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a Rule 30(b)(6) corporate representative may testify beyond personal knowledge at summary judgment for purposes of establishing the on-sale bar under pre-AIA 35 U.S.C. § 102(b), and whether source code constitutes hearsay.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed summary judgment of invalidity under the on-sale bar, holding that 30(b)(6) corporate representative testimony is admissible at summary judgment even when the witness lacks personal knowledge of the facts, and that computer source code describing commands is not hearsay. The decision invalidated patent claims on engine controller reprogramming methods based on prior sales by a predecessor company.
impacts.positiveImplications: Defendants challenging patent validity benefit from expanded evidentiary tools: 30(b)(6) testimony need not be based on personal knowledge and source code is generally admissible as non-hearsay, lowering the burden to prove prior art sales.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face increased invalidity risk as defendants can more easily prove on-sale bar defenses using corporate representative testimony and source code evidence, even when original witnesses are unavailable.

impacts.caseName: Ascendis Pharma A/S v. BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an ITC respondent may obtain a mandatory stay under 28 U.S.C. § 1659(a) of a refiled declaratory judgment action when it missed the statutory deadline for seeking a stay of the original action involving the same parties and issues.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that BioMarin could not obtain a mandatory stay of Ascendis's refiled declaratory judgment action under § 1659(a) after having missed the 30-day deadline for seeking a stay of the original action. The court ruled that allowing a stay of the refiled action would permit gamesmanship and circumvention of the statutory deadline. This preserves Ascendis's ability to litigate its safe harbor defense in district court concurrently with the ITC investigation.
impacts.positiveImplications: Declaratory judgment plaintiffs are protected from tactical delays by ITC respondents who missed statutory stay deadlines; pharmaceutical companies can pursue parallel district court actions asserting safe harbor defenses without mandatory stays.
impacts.negativeImplications: ITC respondents/patent holders lose the ability to cure missed stay deadlines through procedural maneuvers, potentially facing simultaneous proceedings in multiple forums.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent damages expert's report and granting summary judgment of no remedy based on the exclusion.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report and vacated summary judgment for Microsoft, holding that the district court applied too stringent a standard in evaluating the expert's methodology. The court emphasized that damages experts should be given reasonable latitude in quantifying the value of patented technology, particularly in complex networking technology cases. The case is remanded for further proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent plaintiffs benefit from a more permissive standard for admitting damages expert testimony, reducing the risk that technically complex but methodologically sound damages theories will be excluded.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies defending patent cases face greater difficulty excluding damages evidence at summary judgment, potentially increasing settlement pressure and trial exposure.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Patent Trial and Appeal Board correctly construed the term 'encryption information' in claims directed to streaming partly encrypted media content, and whether the prior art rendered the claims obvious under that construction.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the Board's claim construction of 'encryption information' in DivX's streaming media patent, vacated the Board's finding of non-obviousness, and remanded for further proceedings. The court adopted a broader construction that could facilitate Netflix's obviousness challenge to DivX's patent covering adaptive bitrate streaming with partial encryption.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming technology companies benefit from a broader construction of encryption-related patent terms that may facilitate invalidity challenges to patents covering DRM and content protection in streaming systems.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the digital content protection space face risk that narrowly drafted encryption-related claims may be construed more broadly, making them more vulnerable to prior art challenges.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly approved a $125 million class action settlement of claims that the federal judiciary overcharged PACER users by using fees to fund expenses unrelated to the PACER system, and whether incentive awards to class representatives were permissible.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed approval of a $125 million settlement in the nationwide class action alleging the federal judiciary charged excessive PACER fees beyond what was permitted by statute. The court upheld the settlement terms, attorneys' fees, and incentive awards to the three nonprofit class representatives, rejecting objections that the settlement was inadequate and that incentive awards violated Supreme Court precedent.
impacts.positiveImplications: The settlement provides substantial reimbursement to hundreds of thousands of PACER users, including businesses, law firms, and organizations that regularly access court records; validates the use of incentive awards to nonprofit class representatives.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Board governing the exercise of discretion to decline institution of inter partes review proceedings constitute 'general statements of policy' exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b).
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions governing discretionary IPR non-institution decisions are general statements of policy exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking requirements. The court emphasized that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution, the instructions do not bind the Director, and non-institution decisions are statutorily insulated from judicial review.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's retained discretion to decline IPR institution without procedural constraints, preserving patent stability against challenges.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies seeking to challenge patents through IPR face continued uncertainty as the PTO Director retains broad, judicially unreviewable discretion to decline institution without notice-and-comment rulemaking constraints on that discretion.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the International Trade Commission's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in countervailing duty and antidumping investigations is consistent with the governing statute and the common law right of access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of International Trade's holding that the ITC's blanket practice of designating all questionnaire responses as confidential is incompatible with the statute and does not abrogate the common law right of access to judicial records. The decision requires the ITC to make individualized confidentiality determinations rather than automatically classifying all submissions as confidential.
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers, domestic producers, and the public benefit from greater transparency in trade remedy proceedings; competitors and trade associations gain access to information previously shielded by blanket confidentiality designations.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies submitting questionnaire responses in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations face increased risk of disclosure of commercially sensitive business information that was previously automatically protected.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Restaurant Revitalization Fund statute created a money-mandating obligation enforceable under the Tucker Act, entitling eligible restaurants that applied but were not funded to damages for unpaid grants.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed denial of the government's motion to dismiss, holding that the RRF statute's mandatory language ('shall award grants' and 'shall use amounts') created enforceable money-mandating obligations under the Tucker Act. Over 300 restaurants that timely applied but were passed over due to the SBA's processing order (rather than the statutory application-receipt order) may proceed with their damages claims.
impacts.positiveImplications: Small businesses that were denied federal grants due to agency processing errors have a viable path to recover damages; the decision reinforces government accountability when statutes use mandatory grant-making language.
impacts.negativeImplications: The federal government faces potential liability for hundreds of millions in unpaid grants, and agencies must adhere strictly to statutory processing and award sequences in grant programs.
